# Figure description: скв_393.jpg

**Source**: `36572_Smolianova_1984/raw_graphics/Колонки_ скважин/скв_393.jpg`  
**Generated by**: claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## Figure Type

This is a **borehole/well log (геологический разрез скважины)** — a detailed lithological and geophysical well log recorded on a continuous paper strip (karotazhnaya diagrama / каротажная диаграмма). It appears to be a composite well log showing one or more geophysical curves (likely spontaneous potential / SP and/or resistivity curves) plotted against depth, with accompanying lithological and stratigraphic annotations in the margins. The format is characteristic of Soviet-era wireline logging strips used in regional exploration drilling programs.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The log covers a single borehole (скважина), likely one of the exploration/parametric wells drilled as part of the regional survey of map sheets **M-42 and N-42** in northern Kazakhstan. The log appears to extend over a substantial depth range — potentially **several hundred to over 1,000 meters** based on the length of the strip. The title block at the top (partially legible) appears to reference the borehole designation and survey program. Stratigraphic formation names and lithological unit boundaries are annotated in the left and right margins at corresponding depth levels.

---

## Key Content

The central portion of the figure shows **one or two continuous geophysical curves** (wiggly lines running the full length of the log strip) plotted within a gridded track. The curves display:

- **High-amplitude, irregular deflections** in the upper portion of the log, suggesting lithological heterogeneity (interbedded sequences, possibly alternating resistive and conductive layers — e.g., sandstones and clays or carbonates and shales).
- **Progressively lower amplitude and more subdued fluctuations** in the lower portion, possibly indicating more homogeneous or deeper basement/crystalline rock with less contrast between beds.
- A **notable transition zone** roughly in the middle of the log where curve character changes significantly — this likely corresponds to a major stratigraphic or lithological boundary (e.g., sedimentary cover overlying metamorphic or igneous basement).
- The **right-hand curve** (if two tracks are present) may represent a different logging parameter (e.g., resistivity vs. SP), showing complementary but distinct behavior.

Marginal annotations at various depth levels appear to mark **formation tops, lithological descriptions, and possibly core sample intervals**. Horizontal lines across the log correspond to **depth markers** (likely every 10 or 50 meters).

---

## Text and Labels

Due to the low resolution and faded condition of the image, full transcription is not possible, but the following elements are identifiable:

- **Title block (top center):** Partially legible text, likely reading something along the lines of:
  **«РЕК КАРОТОГРАДСКОГО»** or similar — possibly a borehole or survey name. A subtitle line appears to reference the report or survey program.

- **Top-right block:** Contains tabular data — likely borehole metadata (coordinates, elevation, total depth, drilling dates, logging company/operator).

- **Top-left block:** May contain lithological legend or drilling parameters.

- **Left margin:** Repeated annotations at depth intervals — likely formation names (свиты, горизонты) and lithological descriptions (e.g., «глины», «песчаники», «известняки», «кристаллический фундамент»).

- **Right margin:** Similar stratigraphic/lithological notations, possibly including age designations (e.g., K, J, P, C — Cretaceous, Jurassic, Paleogene, Carboniferous).

- **Bottom-right corner:** A small box likely containing the **cartouche** with drafter name, date, and document number.

- **Depth scale:** Runs vertically along the center grid; tick marks appear at regular intervals.

---

## Research Value

This well log provides a **continuous record of subsurface lithology and physical rock properties** through the stratigraphic column intersected by the borehole, serving as a primary ground-truth dataset for the regional seismic and gravity/magnetic surveys conducted under Report 36572. A geologist or AI research assistant would use it to **identify formation tops, estimate depths to basement, and correlate subsurface units across the survey area** — critical for building a 3D structural model of the region. In the context of mineral potential assessment, the log helps characterize the thickness and distribution of sedimentary cover versus crystalline basement, which directly informs targeting of sediment-hosted or basement-related mineralization.
